coredata.dc:title: Determination of carbohydrate content in kenaf degumming wastewater and converting them to carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Xiaolong
Li, Linlin
Liu, Qiulian
Zhang, Yuanming
Han, Guangting
Ben, Haoxi
Zhao, Haiguang
Jiang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The traditional textile degumming process produces abundant wastewater, which contains a lot of monosaccharides and oligosaccharides. It is of great economic and environmental significance to utilize these carbohydrates in high value. In this study, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to analyze the carbohydrate components in kenaf degumming wastewater, and then the production of C-dots using the wastewater was explored. The results showed that the types and content in the degumming wastewater were monosaccharides (glucose, xylose and arabinose) and oligosaccharides (dextran, xylan and araban). The carbohydrate (mainly glucan and xylan) content in wastewater accounted for 91.16 % of the total carbohydrates weight loss in kenaf degumming process. By using hydrolysis and hydrothermal reaction on kenaf degumming wastewater, blue-green carbon dots (C-dots) with good performance were prepared and successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting printing. In particular, the as-prepared C-dots prepared from kenaf degumming wastewater with urea added (WUC-dots) showed an excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and quantum yield (QY) of 2.4 % in aqueous solution. The fluorescent code exhibited a clear outline, excitation-tunable color and good stability, showing a great potential for anti-counterfeiting system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kenaf bast
Degumming wastewater
Carbohydrates
High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
Carbon dots (C-dots)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative determination for the chemical composition of kenaf degumming wastewater
Preparation of fluorescent ink and patterns
Component analyses of kenaf bast and degumming wastewater
Condition optimization and optical properties for C-dots prepared from xylose and glucose
Structure and optical properties of C-dots prepared from kenaf degumming wastewater

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies
Special Foundation of “Taishan Scholar” Construction Program
Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Shandong Province ( YDZX2022156 ), State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies ( FZ2022007 ) and Special Foundation of “Taishan Scholar” Construction Program ( ts20190932 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Component analysis chart flow of kenaf bast and degumming wastewater.

Note: Kenaf-H2SO4, Kenaf-NaOH1, Kenaf-NaOH2 represented the kenaf residues after H2SO4 and NaOH treatment; W-H2SO4 represented the wastewater derived from the 1 wt% H2SO4 treatment, W1 represented the wastewater derived from the first cycle NaOH treatment, W2 represented the wastewater derived from the second cycle NaOH treatment.
(a) TEM image of WUC-dots. (b) Size distribution of the WUC-dots. (c) HRTEM image of the WUC-dots.
(a) The full survey XPS spectra of the WUC-dots. (b-d) The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C l s, N 1s, O 1s of the WUC-dots.

PL spectra of the WC-dots (a) and WUC-dots (b) upon excitation at different wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of the WC-dots, WUC-dots, and fluorescent patterns printed with the WUC-dots (FP-WUC-dots). The black line in Figure a-b was the absorption spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots.
The designed and printed fluorescent patterns on cotton fabric with WUC-dots ink under room light and illumination of 365 and 395 nm.
The content of different carbohydrates in W1 and W2.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: C TCL = (m holocellulose in Kenaf-H2SO4 - m holocellulose in Kenaf-NaOH2)/(m Kenaf-H2SO4 × 20) × 1000.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HPLC results of H2SO4 (4 wt%) hydrolyzed wastewater (H2SO4-HW), WC-dots aqueous solution (WC-dots AS) and WUC-dots aqueous solution (WUC-dots AS).
Atomic percentages of the WUC-dots elements.

head.ce:title: Determination of carbohydrate content in kenaf degumming wastewater and converting them to carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The traditional textile degumming process produces abundant wastewater, which contains a lot of monosaccharides and oligosaccharides. It is of great economic and environmental significance to utilize these carbohydrates in high value. In this study, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to analyze the carbohydrate components in kenaf degumming wastewater, and then the production of C-dots using the wastewater was explored. The results showed that the types and content in the degumming wastewater were monosaccharides (glucose, xylose and arabinose) and oligosaccharides (dextran, xylan and araban). The carbohydrate (mainly glucan and xylan) content in wastewater accounted for 91.16 % of the total carbohydrates weight loss in kenaf degumming process. By using hydrolysis and hydrothermal reaction on kenaf degumming wastewater, blue-green carbon dots (C-dots) with good performance were prepared and successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting printing. In particular, the as-prepared C-dots prepared from kenaf degumming wastewater with urea added (WUC-dots) showed an excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and quantum yield (QY) of 2.4 % in aqueous solution. The fluorescent code exhibited a clear outline, excitation-tunable color and good stability, showing a great potential for anti-counterfeiting system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, environmental pollution has gained more and more public attention [1]; clean production, wastewater treatment and industrial waste reuse have become the focus of research [2]. Bast fiber is one of the raw materials in the textile industry, according to statistics, the global demand for bast fiber such as hemp fiber is growing with rate of 20 % per year [3]. However, bast fibers produce a large amount of wastewater during the degumming process, and the traditional chemical degumming method can produce over 500 ton of wastewater by producing 1 ton of bast fiber [4], which hinders the sustainable development of the bast fiber based textile industry. Therefore, the precise analysis and utilization of the components of degumming wastewater are of great significance to reduce pollution and promote the development of bast fiber based textile industry [5].

As one of biomass raw materials, the plant bast contains cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, pectin and other components [6]. In the degumming process, the chemical components are decomposed or dissolved in the degumming wastewater under acid or alkali conditions [7]. The degumming wastewater contains a lot of organic matter. The main components are hydrolyzed pectin, hemicellulose and cellulose, alkali lignin, etc. [8], and the wastewater treatment is difficult and high cost [9]. At present, the treatment methods of degumming wastewater consisted of physicochemical method, biological method, solar desalination, etc. [10]. The application of physicochemical treatment methods has been well documented over the past two decades because of their ability to remove a variety of suspended and floating matters and toxic compounds from wastewater [11]. However, this method is complex and costly [11,12]. Biological treatment methods involve the utilization of microorganisms, as a single step treatment or in combination with other physical and/or chemical methods [13]. Biological methods for wastewater treatment are considered to be of cost benefit, eco-friendly, and suitable for reduction of the biological oxygen demand (BOD) and chemical oxygen demand (COD) from wastewater [14]. However, the conventional biological processes have not effectively performed for removal of color and recalcitrant compounds from wastewater [11,15]. Solar desalination for the textile industry wastewater treatment has been proved to be an affordable technology. The primary drawback of solar desalination is its low productivity [16]. The organic pollutants in degumming wastewater are mainly monosaccharides, polysaccharides, oligosaccharides, etc. [17] If the carbohydrates in degumming wastewater can be extracted and utilized, the pollution of degumming wastewater will be effectively reduced and additional benefits will be generated [17,18].

The reuse of biomass refining wastewater mainly focuses on alkali recovery and alkali lignin recovery [19] by combustion method [20] and filtration method [12]. Except for alkali and lignin, oligosaccharides dissolved in biomass refining wastewater are high in content, but few studies have reported their high-value utilization [21]. Biopolysaccharides can be used to prepare carbon dots (C-dots) [22], which have the advantages of strong light luminescence, tunable excitation/emission and excellent electron transfer ability of traditional semiconductor quantum dots [17,23], but also effectively overcome the shortcomings of traditional semiconductor quantum dots such as high toxicity and poor biocompatibility [18], and have potential application prospects in many fields such as medicine, chemical industry, biology, energy, materials, printing, etc. [24] Due to the complexity of carbohydrates in wastewater, the preparation methods of C-dots with high conversion and yield are greatly limited [25]. Therefore, it is important to establish a method to analyze the carbohydrate components in degumming wastewater and obtain the accurate data [26].

At present, the carbohydrate analysis of biomass refining wastewater mainly uses phenol-sulfuric acid spectrophotometry to determine the total carbohydrate content, and ion chromatography and gel chromatography to determine the monosaccharide and polysaccharide content [27]. These methods need to extract, separate and purify different carbohydrate components, and the steps are complicated [17]. Currently, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was wildly used in the analysis of carbohydrates in biomass. But, there is no relevant studies conducted to detect the monosaccharides and glycans in degumming wastewater [4].

In this study, the types and content of different carbohydrates in kenaf degumming wastewater were analyzed by HPLC for the first time. A detailed process for the analysis of kenaf degumming wastewater was established. The reliability of the method was verified by analyzing the degradation of kenaf cellulose and hemicellulose. C-dots were prepared via a hydrothermal method and applied in the field of anti-counterfeiting printing, so as to realize the high-value utilization of kenaf degumming wastewater (Fig. 1 ).

Kenaf bast was obtained from Luan city, Anhui Province, China and it was dried in a blast oven for 6 h before the experiment. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phenethyl alcohol (C8H10O), ammonium oxalate [(NH4)2C2O4] and urea (CH₄N₂O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Xylose, arabinose, cellose and glucose were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., China and were chromatographically pure.

Kenaf bast (average length of 1–2 cm) was ball milled into 60–80 mesh powder, then 5 g was taken and immersed in 1 wt% H2SO4 (kenaf bast: H2SO4 = 1:20, w/v) for 1 h at 60 °C. The wastewater produced by water bath was collected, named W-H2SO4. Finally, the kenaf bast was collected and further washed to neutral (pH = 7) and dried.

In the degumming process (two-step alkaline cooking method), kenaf bast was first boiled with 1 wt% NaOH (kenaf bast: NaOH = 1:20, w/v) for 1 h, the wastewater produced by cooking was collected, named W1, and the kenaf bast was rinsed with water to pH = 7. Subsequently, the kenaf bast was boiled again under the same conditions for 3 h, and the wastewater produced by cooking was collected, named W2. The detailed collection process of kenaf degumming wastewater was schematically shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination for the chemical composition of kenaf degumming wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical component content of kenaf bast was determined and calculated according to National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL)/TP-510-42,618. In order to reduce the experimental error, kenaf bast was extracted with benzene-ethanol solution (benzene: ethanol = 2:1, v/v) for 3 h, and the lipid wax in kenaf bast was removed before test.

The content of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin were determined by HPLC. First, the dried samples were hydrolyzed in 72 wt% H2SO4 for 1 h, then diluted with water to 4 wt% H2SO4 concentration, treated in an autoclave (wastewater sample volume 150 mL) at 121 °C for 1 h, and the supernatant was tested by HPLC and UV–visible absorption spectra. The content of cellulose and hemicellulose was obtained by HPLC, and the content of acid-soluble lignin was detected by UV–visible absorption at 320 nm. The remaining liquid was filtered with a sand core funnel. The solids on the sand core funnel were acid-insoluble lignin. Each group of samples had 3 parallel samples, and each sample was measured 3 times to ensure data accuracy.

Determination of monosaccharide content: the monosaccharides in black liquor were determined by HPLC. First, the pH of the black solution was adjusted to 2, and centrifuged (15,000 r/min, 20 min), and the supernatant was filtered for testing. HPLC conditions: chromatographic column Bio. Rad Aminex HPX87H, eluent 0.002 mol/L H2SO4, flow rate 0.6 mL/min, column temperature 50 °C, sample amount 10 μL, differential refraction detector (RID, 45 °C).

Determination of oligosaccharides: W1 or W2 (50 mL, lignin precipitation treatment) was added with H2SO4 (72 wt%) to pH = 1.7 (4 wt% H2SO4), and then hydrolyzed (121 °C, 1 h). The hydrolyzed wastewater was centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered (filtration film pore size 0.22 μm), and then tested by HPLC under the same conditions as above. The content of oligosaccharides in degumming wastewater was calculated according to the measured monosaccharide content after hydrolysis. The measured glucose content was multiplied by a coefficient (0.9) to obtain the content of glucan, and the content of xylose and arabinose was multiplied by a coefficient (0.88) to obtain the content of xylan and araban, respectively.

Extraction of acid evolution lignin (AEL) and determination of lignin complex carbohydrates (LCC): H2SO4 (10 wt%) was used to adjust the pH of kenaf degumming wastewater to 2.5–3, and the water was bathed at 65 °C for 20 min, and then stood at room temperature for 3 h. Finally, the lignin was obtained by vacuum filtration and freeze-drying. The content of LCC in AEL was determined by monosaccharides. The process was as follows: 4–6 mg AEL was hydrolyzed with 0.125 mL H2SO4 (72 wt%) for 5 min, then diluted with 1.35 mL water, and finally hydrolyzed at 105 °C for 2.5 h. The hydrolyzed wastewater was centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered (filtration film pore size 0.22 μm), and then tested by HPLC under the same conditions as above.

H2SO4 (4 wt%) was added to W2 and hydrolyzed in a hydrothermal reactor (121 °C, 1 h). The hydrolyzed liquid was centrifuged and the supernatant was filtered. Various amounts of urea were added to the supernatant (urea: carbohydrates = 0:1, 2:1, 4:1, 6:1, 8:1, 10:1, 12:1, 14:1, M/M). The mixed solution (10 mL) was placed in a hydrothermal reactor and reacted in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. Finally, the mixed solution was dialyzed for 24 h and freeze-dried. The dried powder was collected and used for further characterizations and security code fabrication.

The morphologies and structures of the as-prepared C-dots were measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100F, Japan). The absorption property of the C-dots was characterized by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Japan); the fluorescence spectrum, fluorescence lifetime and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the purified C-dots were characterized by steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Britain), assisted with an integration sphere. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS Supra, Japan) were used to characterize the surface chemical structure of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent ink and patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent ink consisted of fatty alcohol polyoxyethylene ether (AEO, 1 wt%, surfactant), sodium polyacrylate (0.005 wt%, thickener), ethylene glycol (EG, 1 wt%, moisturizing and solubilizing reagent), C-dots prepared from kenaf degumming wastewater with urea added (WUC-dots, 0.35 wt%) and water (97.645 wt%). After magnetic stirring for 4 h, the obtained mixture was filtered by a 0.45 μm filter and further filtered by a 0.22 μm filter to obtain fluorescent ink. Finally, the fluorescent ink was printed on cotton fabric by an inkjet printing machine (Canon, HFTX-P4290C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component analyses of kenaf bast and degumming wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: It is very important to clearly understand the degradation pathway of kenaf chemical composition and finally find the content of different carbohydrates in the wastewater. Fig. 2 showed the analysis process on kenaf bast and the degumming residues as well as wastewater.

The detailed data of kenaf bast chemical composition content, different carbohydrate content was present in Tables S1-S3. Due to the low monosaccharide and polysaccharide content in W-H2SO4, we focused on the monosaccharide and polysaccharide content in W1 and W2 (Table S2). The content of monosaccharides and polysaccharides in Tables S2 and S3 was summarized in Table 1 . By analyzing Table S1-S3, it could be found that cellulose (0.22 g), hemicellulose (0.21 g) and lignin (0.17 g) were degraded and the total carbohydrate loss (TCL) could be calculated to be 4.98 g/L (Table 1) in W1 and W2.

The content of polysaccharides in kenaf degumming wastewater was higher than that of monosaccharides. The content of glucan and xylan in wastewater was the highest, 1.19 ± 0.20 g/L and 1.48 ± 0.15 g/L, respectively. The overall carbohydrate content was 4.54 ± 0.78 g/L, and the TCL in the degumming process was 4.98 g/L in wastewater. The detectable carbohydrate content accounted for 91.16 % of the TCL in kenaf degumming process, indicating that it was accurate to determine the carbohydrate content of the kenaf degumming wastewater by HPLC. The data also showed that the total oligosaccharide content accounted for 70.7 % of the total carbohydrate content in kenaf degumming wastewater. The data gave the guidance that a pre-hydrolysis process should be conducted in order to convert these carbohydrates to C-dots.

HPLC was also performed on kenaf degumming wastewater after C-dots preparation, as shown in Table 2 . The data showed that more than 93 % monosaccharides were transformed to C-dots during the reaction, the carbohydrate content was significantly reduced. The above data implied that this pathway was effective to reduce the COD value in wastewater and had high ability to produce high value-added C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization and optical properties for C-dots prepared from xylose and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: We used the commercial glucose and xylose as precursor for the synthesis of the C-dots. The C-dots produced by the glucose and xylose were named as GC-dots and XC-dots. As shown in Fig. S2a, by fixing the reaction time of 6 h, the relative PL intensity of the C-dots produced by GC-dots increased to maximum at 180 °C. For the XC-dots, after the fast increase of the relative PL intensity until 180 °C, the PL intensity still showed a slightly increase when the reaction temperature increased to 190 °C. Thus, we chose the 180 °C for the growth of the C-dots. At this temperature, the 6-h reaction time gave the maximum PL intensity (Fig. S2b). Based on these data, for the synthesis of the WC-dots or WUC-dots, we set up the reaction time of 6 h and reaction temperature of 180 °C. Both types of the C-dots produced by glucose or xylcose showed a strong excitation-dependent PL spectrum (Fig. S3). With the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 350 nm, the fluorescence emission peak gradually shifted to long wavelengths. This was because that the synthesized C-dots didn't have a highly crystalline core, showing a molecular-like PL behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of C-dots prepared from kenaf degumming wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and size of the as-prepared C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 3a-b, the WUC-dots exhibited typically spherical shape with an average particle size of 5.0 ± 1.0 nm. The measured d-spacing was around 0.26 nm (Fig. 3c), matching well with that of the graphite carbon [28,29]. The clear lattice also indicated the high crystalline structure in C-dots because of the high temperature carbonization [30].

The surface chemical composition of the WUC-dots was analyzed by XPS, and the results were shown in Table 3 and Fig. 4 . Based on the full survey of XPS (Fig. 4a), the as-prepared WUC-dots consisted of C, N, and O elements, with atomic percentages of 33.79 %, 9.04 %, and 41.79 %, respectively. The presence of the Na and S might be the residence of the NaOH and H2SO4 in the wastewater. The spectrum of the C 1 s could be demonstrated into three bonds located at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.6 eV, assigned to C − C/C=C, C − N/C − O, and CO bands (Fig. 4b) [31,32]. The spectrum of the N 1 s could be demonstrated into three bonds located at 399.6 eV and 401.2 eV, assigned to pyrrole N and graphite N bands (Fig. 4c) [31–34]. Fig. 4d showed the O1s of the C-dots, with peaks of 532.0 eV, 531.0 eV, and 535.6 eV, attributed to C-OH/C-O-C, CO, and CO, respectively [30,35,36]. Consistently, the FT-IR results indicated that the WC-dots sample had functional groups of C=O/C=C, C-C/C-H on the surface, while the WUC-dots sample had obvious functional groups of C=O/C=C, C-C/C-H, CN, and C-O-C. New C-C/C-H and C-O-C peaks appeared compared to that of urea (Fig. S4). According to the XPS and FT-IR analysis results, it could be concluded that the surface of WUC dots contained -NH2, -OH and CO, which enabled the good solubility of the C-dots in aqueous solution.

We produced the C-dots using kenaf degumming wastewater, and the results were shown in Fig. S5. At 180 °C, with the increase of reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of WC-dots increased, and the PL intensity didn't show a significant variation after 6 h reaction (Fig. S5a). This trend indicated that 6-h reaction provided the optimized C-dots concentration. We also studied the urea effect on the PL intensity of the C-dots. As shown in Fig. S5b, the best WUC-dots were prepared with the molar ratio of 10:1 (Murea/Mcarbohydrates) under the conditions of reaction temperature 180 °C and reaction time 6 h.

The as-prepared WC-dots and WUC-dots had a typical absorption below 500 nm, which was similar as those of the C-dots produced by glucose or xylose (Fig. S6). The as-prepared WC-dots and WUC-dots had the excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths in the range of 290–400 nm (Fig. 5a-b), which was also similar as that of the C-dots produced by glucose or xylose. This was because the wastewater had the similar content as glucose or xylose, leading to similar structure of the C-dots. Usually the excitation-dependent PL behavior in C-dots was due to the low crystalline core structure and unpacked surface functional groups [37], leading to excitation-dependent energy states in C-dots. In this work, it was more likely due to the unpacked surface functional groups as we found highly crystalline core structure of the C-dots benefited from high-temperature hydrothermal reaction. We did not find a significant variation between WC-dots and WUC-dots, indicating that the adding of urea didn't lead to the red-shift of the PL peak. While the QY of the WUC-dots had a three-time higher value compared to the WC-dots (Table S4), indicating that the urea could decrease the surface traps. Consistently, the lifetime of the WUC-dots was higher than that of the WC-dots (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. S7, we didn't see any of PL intensity and peak position variations for the WUC-dots after stored at 4 °C for 3 months. These results indicated that we could produce the C-dots using the kenaf degumming wastewater.

Zeta potential curve indicated that the surface of WUC-dots was negatively charged and had good stability in aqueous solution, which also offered the C-dots very good water-solubility (Fig. S8). WUC-dots ink was used to print fluorescent patterns on cotton fabric. As shown in Fig. 6 , there was no any visible pattern in the fabric at room light, but a clear blue fluorescent pattern could be seen under the illumination of 365 nm and 395 nm. The patterns had blue-purple color under 365 nm illumination and blue-green color under 395 nm illumination, because of the excitation dependent PL behavior of the WUC-dots. In addition, the WUC-dots did not affect the softness of the cotton fabric. The fluorescent patterns showed a QY of 2.6 % in the solid-state (Table S4), slightly higher than that dispersed in solution (QY of 2.4 %). This could be due to the high-temperature (160 °C) post-treatment after printing. This result indicated that we could fabricate a flexible fluorescent code for multiple-level anti-counterfeiting.

In this work, a comprehensive analysis of kenaf bast before and after degumming process was conducted, by combining wet end chemistry and HPLC, the degraded carbohydrates including monosaccharides and oligosaccharides which dissolved in degumming wastewater were preciously detected. It was found that 91.16 % of the total carbohydrates weight loss in kenaf degumming process could be determined using this method. And the results showed that more than 70 % of the carbohydrates were oligosaccharides. Subsequently, blue-green C-dots with good performance were prepared by using hydrolysis and hydrothermal reaction on the degumming wastewater, which were successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting printing. In particular, the as-prepared WUC-dots showed an excitation-dependent PL spectrum and QY of 2.4 % in aqueous solution. The fluorescent code exhibited a clear outline, excitation-tunable color and good stability, showing a great potential for anti-counterfeiting system.

Xiaolong Qiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Linlin Li: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Qiulian Liu: Data curation. Yuanming Zhang: Resources. Guangting Han: Resources. Haoxi Ben: Funding acquisition. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Wei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Shandong Province (YDZX2022156), State Key Laboratory of New Textile Materials and Advanced Processing Technologies (FZ2022007) and Special Foundation of “Taishan Scholar” Construction Program (ts20190932).

The authors are responsible for the ideas, methods, data and conclusions in the paper. The authors hold independent opinions on the issues studied and are responsible for the reasonableness and reliability of those opinions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.130952.
